My background is not being overlooked, making it difficult to move forward.

I went back to school and got a degree, aiming for corporate or professional work.
It's more like a life sentence than a four-year sentence.
The felony conviction seems to knock me out of the ballpark when applying for jobs.
I don't get callbacks a lot because of that issue.
I interview extremely well, and it feels like the job is offered halfway through the interview, but when it comes to signing the paperwork, I disclose my felony, and it's like night and day.
I get the vibe that the job is offered almost halfway through the interview because, you know, they start telling me about what I'll be doing and what I'll be getting.
In situations where I don't know about it, I don't know about, I mean, if I'm not considered because of that, I probably-- Um, you know, that sucks too.
Black and white. It just stops right there. "I'm sorry. I can't really do much for you. I-- You have to go," you know, and it's—

When it comes to sign on the paperwork and I believe in honesty, so I tell them, you know, hey, look, this is what you're gonna find, you know, and, um, try to explain it. It's just-it's almost like night and day.
Most of the times, I interview extremely well.

The ones that really count are-are-are, you know, are kind of just, you know, uh, making me so angry and, you know, kind of spilling over into the rest of my life or the ones that I get face face, especially after a really good interview.
There are also times where I get an interview through some sort of connection or something, or like a buddy of mine or a family member will send me on an interview.
Sometimes they ask and, you know, I'll say something like, you know, "We'll discuss at interview".
Sometimes, there are situations where if I probably put that, I don't get a callback and those situations I don't know about.
It seems like it's a done deal, you just have to go through the motion of having the interview.
I don't have to fill out an application for interviews through connections; they just give you the interview.
After a great interview, when it comes to signing paperwork, my past conviction, which was non-violent and possession-related, becomes an issue.
The conviction was not violent or theft-related, but possession-related, and it happened seven or six years ago.
The conviction was non-violent and not a danger to others.
It's really frustrating because-
It's almost like I don't wanna give up, but it's like, I'm not given any other choice, you know?
If I disclose my history, I might get booted out right away.
Probably get booted out right away.
It's frustrating to get through a great interview, knowing I'm good for the job, and then get booted out.
If they get past it, then now I know that there's an actual intention by the hiring people to hire me.
If it's gonna make things more efficient, like weeding out the things, not wasting my time, then it's better.
A lot of the people that don't care about my criminal history are not the sort of jobs that I really wanna be doing.

Not like you're working for the government or for a bank or, you know, the FBI where- those are not things you're applying for. They're jobs where you would wonder why a-a non-violent felony would even be an issue.
Be an issue.
Anybody that I might tell that to that doesn't care are probably not jobs that are worth going to get the interview.

If I want to work at a moving company and I tell them off the bat, you know, from the gate, I tell them I have a felony conviction, just so you know before the interview and they don't really mind. I mean, that's great, you know, but at the same time, I don't- I'm not looking to be a mover.
I'm not looking to be a mover or a burger flipper or something like that. Not that there's anything wrong with it, but I feel like I have a little bit more to offer.
It's like being caught between a rock and a hard place.
I have done, and my eyes have, you know, just become like mush after all the reading I've done on this situation.
That's what it all boils down to is that I have a class one felony-

I found out that I have the type of felony that's expungable, I don't even have the type that's sealable. I have apparently the type that has to go on the governor's desk next to death row, you know, inmates or the candidates for a governor's pardon.

That needs to be, if any, you know, options, the only option I have is for governor's clemency and I can't figure out why because it's-it's, I mean, I just, I maybe there is a loophole somewhere, I don't know.
I've looked into a few groups that are supposedly helping convicted felons get work.
I think what I saw was a whole bunch of dead end jobs.
I found it really hard to get in and to get an interview with a counselor to match me up with a job.
It just didn't seem like a type of place that was willing to help.
The other thing that I saw was that it was very politically charged.
I saw that there was a lot of racial favor.
I didn't think that I was allowed to get an interview right away with one of those counselors that they offer.
I think that those types of groups are cool if they work. I just haven't had any luck working through that group.

detail: Having trouble dealing with my criminal history and trying to stay on the right path.
My background is not being overlooked, making it difficult to move forward.
My record has a felony conviction on it.
I hadn't anticipated the impact of the felony conviction; at the time, avoiding jail seemed like a relief.
I went back to school and got a degree, aiming for corporate or professional work.
It's more like a life sentence than a four-year sentence.
The felony conviction seems to knock me out of the ballpark when applying for jobs.
I don't get callbacks a lot because of that issue.
I interview extremely well, and it feels like the job is offered halfway through the interview, but when it comes to signing the paperwork, I disclose my felony, and it's like night and day.
I get the vibe that the job is offered almost halfway through the interview because, you know, they start telling me about what I'll be doing and what I'll be getting.
In situations where I don't know about it, I don't know about, I mean, if I'm not considered because of that, I probably-- Um, you know, that sucks too.
Black and white. It just stops right there. "I'm sorry. I can't really do much for you. I-- You have to go," you know, and it's—

When it comes to sign on the paperwork and I believe in honesty, so I tell them, you know, hey, look, this is what you're gonna find, you know, and, um, try to explain it. It's just-it's almost like night and day.
Most of the times, I interview extremely well.

The ones that really count are-are-are, you know, are kind of just, you know, uh, making me so angry and, you know, kind of spilling over into the rest of my life or the ones that I get face face, especially after a really good interview.
There are also times where I get an interview through some sort of connection or something, or like a buddy of mine or a family member will send me on an interview.
Sometimes they ask and, you know, I'll say something like, you know, "We'll discuss at interview".
Sometimes, there are situations where if I probably put that, I don't get a callback and those situations I don't know about.
It seems like it's a done deal, you just have to go through the motion of having the interview.
I don't have to fill out an application for interviews through connections; they just give you the interview.
After a great interview, when it comes to signing paperwork, my past conviction, which was non-violent and possession-related, becomes an issue.
The conviction was not violent or theft-related, but possession-related, and it happened seven or six years ago.
The conviction was non-violent and not a danger to others.
It's really frustrating because-
It's almost like I don't wanna give up, but it's like, I'm not given any other choice, you know?
If I disclose my history, I might get booted out right away.
Probably get booted out right away.
It's frustrating to get through a great interview, knowing I'm good for the job, and then get booted out.
If they get past it, then now I know that there's an actual intention by the hiring people to hire me.
If it's gonna make things more efficient, like weeding out the things, not wasting my time, then it's better.
A lot of the people that don't care about my criminal history are not the sort of jobs that I really wanna be doing.

Not like you're working for the government or for a bank or, you know, the FBI where- those are not things you're applying for. They're jobs where you would wonder why a-a non-violent felony would even be an issue.
Be an issue.
Anybody that I might tell that to that doesn't care are probably not jobs that are worth going to get the interview.

If I want to work at a moving company and I tell them off the bat, you know, from the gate, I tell them I have a felony conviction, just so you know before the interview and they don't really mind. I mean, that's great, you know, but at the same time, I don't- I'm not looking to be a mover.
I'm not looking to be a mover or a burger flipper or something like that. Not that there's anything wrong with it, but I feel like I have a little bit more to offer.
It's like being caught between a rock and a hard place.
I have done, and my eyes have, you know, just become like mush after all the reading I've done on this situation.
That's what it all boils down to is that I have a class one felony-

I found out that I have the type of felony that's expungable, I don't even have the type that's sealable. I have apparently the type that has to go on the governor's desk next to death row, you know, inmates or the candidates for a governor's pardon.

That needs to be, if any, you know, options, the only option I have is for governor's clemency and I can't figure out why because it's-it's, I mean, I just, I maybe there is a loophole somewhere, I don't know.
I've looked into a few groups that are supposedly helping convicted felons get work.
I think what I saw was a whole bunch of dead end jobs.
I found it really hard to get in and to get an interview with a counselor to match me up with a job.
It just didn't seem like a type of place that was willing to help.
The other thing that I saw was that it was very politically charged.
I saw that there was a lot of racial favor.
I didn't think that I was allowed to get an interview right away with one of those counselors that they offer.
I think that those types of groups are cool if they work. I just haven't had any luck working through that group.
At some point, I probably don't even have to report it, you know, and I have that going for me.
After the 9/11 attacks, many companies blindly implemented policies affecting criminal background checks, and there's no sure way to verify the accuracy of these reports.

Blindly, people have implemented those types of measures and they don't have any efficiency with them because they don't- you know, I mean, everybody is offering background checks now and stuff like that.
I'm afraid of is that people are implementing all these security security measures, but they don't really know if the reports are even accurate.
Experienced racism and prejudice due to being Middle Eastern.
There is a lot stacked up against me.
Jobs are scarce, and it's all about who you know.
We live in a very politically charged city.

It's more like a life sentence than a four-year sentence.
The felony conviction seems to knock me out of the ballpark when applying for jobs.
I don't get callbacks a lot because of that issue.
I interview extremely well, and it feels like the job is offered halfway through the interview, but when it comes to signing the paperwork, I disclose my felony, and it's like night and day.
I get the vibe that the job is offered almost halfway through the interview because, you know, they start telling me about what I'll be doing and what I'll be getting.
In situations where I don't know about it, I don't know about, I mean, if I'm not considered because of that, I probably-- Um, you know, that sucks too.

The ones that really count are-are-are, you know, are kind of just, you know, uh, making me so angry and, you know, kind of spilling over into the rest of my life or the ones that I get face face, especially after a really good interview.

detail: Having trouble dealing with my criminal history and trying to stay on the right path.
My background is not being overlooked, making it difficult to move forward.
My record has a felony conviction on it.
I hadn't anticipated the impact of the felony conviction; at the time, avoiding jail seemed like a relief.
I went back to school and got a degree, aiming for corporate or professional work.
It's more like a life sentence than a four-year sentence.
The felony conviction seems to knock me out of the ballpark when applying for jobs.
I don't get callbacks a lot because of that issue.
I interview extremely well, and it feels like the job is offered halfway through the interview, but when it comes to signing the paperwork, I disclose my felony, and it's like night and day.
I get the vibe that the job is offered almost halfway through the interview because, you know, they start telling me about what I'll be doing and what I'll be getting.
In situations where I don't know about it, I don't know about, I mean, if I'm not considered because of that, I probably-- Um, you know, that sucks too.
Black and white. It just stops right there. "I'm sorry. I can't really do much for you. I-- You have to go," you know, and it's—

When it comes to sign on the paperwork and I believe in honesty, so I tell them, you know, hey, look, this is what you're gonna find, you know, and, um, try to explain it. It's just-it's almost like night and day.
Most of the times, I interview extremely well.

The ones that really count are-are-are, you know, are kind of just, you know, uh, making me so angry and, you know, kind of spilling over into the rest of my life or the ones that I get face face, especially after a really good interview.
There are also times where I get an interview through some sort of connection or something, or like a buddy of mine or a family member will send me on an interview.
Sometimes they ask and, you know, I'll say something like, you know, "We'll discuss at interview".
Sometimes, there are situations where if I probably put that, I don't get a callback and those situations I don't know about.
It seems like it's a done deal, you just have to go through the motion of having the interview.
I don't have to fill out an application for interviews through connections; they just give you the interview.
After a great interview, when it comes to signing paperwork, my past conviction, which was non-violent and possession-related, becomes an issue.
The conviction was not violent or theft-related, but possession-related, and it happened seven or six years ago.
The conviction was non-violent and not a danger to others.
It's really frustrating because-
It's almost like I don't wanna give up, but it's like, I'm not given any other choice, you know?
If I disclose my history, I might get booted out right away.
Probably get booted out right away.
It's frustrating to get through a great interview, knowing I'm good for the job, and then get booted out.
If they get past it, then now I know that there's an actual intention by the hiring people to hire me.
If it's gonna make things more efficient, like weeding out the things, not wasting my time, then it's better.
A lot of the people that don't care about my criminal history are not the sort of jobs that I really wanna be doing.

Not like you're working for the government or for a bank or, you know, the FBI where- those are not things you're applying for. They're jobs where you would wonder why a-a non-violent felony would even be an issue.
Be an issue.
Anybody that I might tell that to that doesn't care are probably not jobs that are worth going to get the interview.

If I want to work at a moving company and I tell them off the bat, you know, from the gate, I tell them I have a felony conviction, just so you know before the interview and they don't really mind. I mean, that's great, you know, but at the same time, I don't- I'm not looking to be a mover.
I'm not looking to be a mover or a burger flipper or something like that. Not that there's anything wrong with it, but I feel like I have a little bit more to offer.
It's like being caught between a rock and a hard place.
I have done, and my eyes have, you know, just become like mush after all the reading I've done on this situation.
That's what it all boils down to is that I have a class one felony-

I found out that I have the type of felony that's expungable, I don't even have the type that's sealable. I have apparently the type that has to go on the governor's desk next to death row, you know, inmates or the candidates for a governor's pardon.

That needs to be, if any, you know, options, the only option I have is for governor's clemency and I can't figure out why because it's-it's, I mean, I just, I maybe there is a loophole somewhere, I don't know.
I've looked into a few groups that are supposedly helping convicted felons get work.
I think what I saw was a whole bunch of dead end jobs.
I found it really hard to get in and to get an interview with a counselor to match me up with a job.
It just didn't seem like a type of place that was willing to help.
The other thing that I saw was that it was very politically charged.
I saw that there was a lot of racial favor.
I didn't think that I was allowed to get an interview right away with one of those counselors that they offer.
I think that those types of groups are cool if they work. I just haven't had any luck working through that group.
At some point, I probably don't even have to report it, you know, and I have that going for me.
After the 9/11 attacks, many companies blindly implemented policies affecting criminal background checks, and there's no sure way to verify the accuracy of these reports.

Blindly, people have implemented those types of measures and they don't have any efficiency with them because they don't- you know, I mean, everybody is offering background checks now and stuff like that.
I'm afraid of is that people are implementing all these security security measures, but they don't really know if the reports are even accurate.
I have run my own background check to see what's in there.
Experienced racism and prejudice due to being Middle Eastern.
There is a lot stacked up against me.
Jobs are scarce, and it's all about who you know.
We live in a very politically charged city.
Feeling discouraged is a dangerous area for me.
Despite all the good things I have going for me, the only thing that's loud and clear are the things that I don't have going for me.

detail: Having trouble dealing with my criminal history and trying to stay on the right path.
My background is not being overlooked, making it difficult to move forward.
My record has a felony conviction on it.
I hadn't anticipated the impact of the felony conviction; at the time, avoiding jail seemed like a relief.
I went back to school and got a degree, aiming for corporate or professional work.
It's more like a life sentence than a four-year sentence.
The felony conviction seems to knock me out of the ballpark when applying for jobs.
I don't get callbacks a lot because of that issue.
I interview extremely well, and it feels like the job is offered halfway through the interview, but when it comes to signing the paperwork, I disclose my felony, and it's like night and day.
I get the vibe that the job is offered almost halfway through the interview because, you know, they start telling me about what I'll be doing and what I'll be getting.
In situations where I don't know about it, I don't know about, I mean, if I'm not considered because of that, I probably-- Um, you know, that sucks too.
Black and white. It just stops right there. "I'm sorry. I can't really do much for you. I-- You have to go," you know, and it's—

When it comes to sign on the paperwork and I believe in honesty, so I tell them, you know, hey, look, this is what you're gonna find, you know, and, um, try to explain it. It's just-it's almost like night and day.
Most of the times, I interview extremely well.

The ones that really count are-are-are, you know, are kind of just, you know, uh, making me so angry and, you know, kind of spilling over into the rest of my life or the ones that I get face face, especially after a really good interview.
There are also times where I get an interview through some sort of connection or something, or like a buddy of mine or a family member will send me on an interview.
Sometimes they ask and, you know, I'll say something like, you know, "We'll discuss at interview".
Sometimes, there are situations where if I probably put that, I don't get a callback and those situations I don't know about.
It seems like it's a done deal, you just have to go through the motion of having the interview.
I don't have to fill out an application for interviews through connections; they just give you the interview.
After a great interview, when it comes to signing paperwork, my past conviction, which was non-violent and possession-related, becomes an issue.
The conviction was not violent or theft-related, but possession-related, and it happened seven or six years ago.
The conviction was non-violent and not a danger to others.
It's really frustrating because-
It's almost like I don't wanna give up, but it's like, I'm not given any other choice, you know?
If I disclose my history, I might get booted out right away.
Probably get booted out right away.
It's frustrating to get through a great interview, knowing I'm good for the job, and then get booted out.
If they get past it, then now I know that there's an actual intention by the hiring people to hire me.
If it's gonna make things more efficient, like weeding out the things, not wasting my time, then it's better.
A lot of the people that don't care about my criminal history are not the sort of jobs that I really wanna be doing.

Not like you're working for the government or for a bank or, you know, the FBI where- those are not things you're applying for. They're jobs where you would wonder why a-a non-violent felony would even be an issue.
Be an issue.
Anybody that I might tell that to that doesn't care are probably not jobs that are worth going to get the interview.

If I want to work at a moving company and I tell them off the bat, you know, from the gate, I tell them I have a felony conviction, just so you know before the interview and they don't really mind. I mean, that's great, you know, but at the same time, I don't- I'm not looking to be a mover.
I'm not looking to be a mover or a burger flipper or something like that. Not that there's anything wrong with it, but I feel like I have a little bit more to offer.
It's like being caught between a rock and a hard place.
I have done, and my eyes have, you know, just become like mush after all the reading I've done on this situation.
That's what it all boils down to is that I have a class one felony-

I found out that I have the type of felony that's expungable, I don't even have the type that's sealable. I have apparently the type that has to go on the governor's desk next to death row, you know, inmates or the candidates for a governor's pardon.

That needs to be, if any, you know, options, the only option I have is for governor's clemency and I can't figure out why because it's-it's, I mean, I just, I maybe there is a loophole somewhere, I don't know.

I think what I saw was a whole bunch of dead end jobs.

I saw that there was a lot of racial favor.
I didn't think that I was allowed to get an interview right away with one of those counselors that they offer.
I think that those types of groups are cool if they work. I just haven't had any luck working through that group.
At some point, I probably don't even have to report it, you know, and I have that going for me.

I have run my own background check to see what's in there.

I recently had an interview through my brother's connection, which went well until the background check paperwork was presented.
I know that I am a new guy, but they don't, so I think society's a lot less forgiving.

I need to be centered to maintain composure and get ahead.

harm_effect: It's overwhelming and causing anger and depression to resurface.
The ones that really count are making me so angry and spilling over into the rest of my life.
necessity_to_improve: I don't want to fall back.
detail: Having trouble dealing with my criminal history and trying to stay on the right path.
My background is not being overlooked, making it difficult to move forward.
My record has a felony conviction on it.
I hadn't anticipated the impact of the felony conviction; at the time, avoiding jail seemed like a relief.
I went back to school and got a degree, aiming for corporate or professional work.
It's more like a life sentence than a four-year sentence.
The felony conviction seems to knock me out of the ballpark when applying for jobs.
I don't get callbacks a lot because of that issue.
I interview extremely well, and it feels like the job is offered halfway through the interview, but when it comes to signing the paperwork, I disclose my felony, and it's like night and day.
I get the vibe that the job is offered almost halfway through the interview because, you know, they start telling me about what I'll be doing and what I'll be getting.
In situations where I don't know about it, I don't know about, I mean, if I'm not considered because of that, I probably-- Um, you know, that sucks too.
Black and white. It just stops right there. "I'm sorry. I can't really do much for you. I-- You have to go," you know, and it's—

When it comes to sign on the paperwork and I believe in honesty, so I tell them, you know, hey, look, this is what you're gonna find, you know, and, um, try to explain it. It's just-it's almost like night and day.
Most of the times, I interview extremely well.

The ones that really count are-are-are, you know, are kind of just, you know, uh, making me so angry and, you know, kind of spilling over into the rest of my life or the ones that I get face face, especially after a really good interview.
There are also times where I get an interview through some sort of connection or something, or like a buddy of mine or a family member will send me on an interview.
Sometimes they ask and, you know, I'll say something like, you know, "We'll discuss at interview".
Sometimes, there are situations where if I probably put that, I don't get a callback and those situations I don't know about.
It seems like it's a done deal, you just have to go through the motion of having the interview.
I don't have to fill out an application for interviews through connections; they just give you the interview.
After a great interview, when it comes to signing paperwork, my past conviction, which was non-violent and possession-related, becomes an issue.
The conviction was not violent or theft-related, but possession-related, and it happened seven or six years ago.
The conviction was non-violent and not a danger to others.
It's really frustrating because-
It's almost like I don't wanna give up, but it's like, I'm not given any other choice, you know?
If I disclose my history, I might get booted out right away.
Probably get booted out right away.
It's frustrating to get through a great interview, knowing I'm good for the job, and then get booted out.
If they get past it, then now I know that there's an actual intention by the hiring people to hire me.
If it's gonna make things more efficient, like weeding out the things, not wasting my time, then it's better.
A lot of the people that don't care about my criminal history are not the sort of jobs that I really wanna be doing.

Not like you're working for the government or for a bank or, you know, the FBI where- those are not things you're applying for. They're jobs where you would wonder why a-a non-violent felony would even be an issue.
Be an issue.
Anybody that I might tell that to that doesn't care are probably not jobs that are worth going to get the interview.

If I want to work at a moving company and I tell them off the bat, you know, from the gate, I tell them I have a felony conviction, just so you know before the interview and they don't really mind. I mean, that's great, you know, but at the same time, I don't- I'm not looking to be a mover.
I'm not looking to be a mover or a burger flipper or something like that. Not that there's anything wrong with it, but I feel like I have a little bit more to offer.
It's like being caught between a rock and a hard place.
I have done, and my eyes have, you know, just become like mush after all the reading I've done on this situation.
That's what it all boils down to is that I have a class one felony-

I found out that I have the type of felony that's expungable, I don't even have the type that's sealable. I have apparently the type that has to go on the governor's desk next to death row, you know, inmates or the candidates for a governor's pardon.

That needs to be, if any, you know, options, the only option I have is for governor's clemency and I can't figure out why because it's-it's, I mean, I just, I maybe there is a loophole somewhere, I don't know.
I've looked into a few groups that are supposedly helping convicted felons get work.
I think what I saw was a whole bunch of dead end jobs.
I found it really hard to get in and to get an interview with a counselor to match me up with a job.
It just didn't seem like a type of place that was willing to help.
The other thing that I saw was that it was very politically charged.
I saw that there was a lot of racial favor.
I didn't think that I was allowed to get an interview right away with one of those counselors that they offer.
I think that those types of groups are cool if they work. I just haven't had any luck working through that group.
At some point, I probably don't even have to report it, you know, and I have that going for me.
After the 9/11 attacks, many companies blindly implemented policies affecting criminal background checks, and there's no sure way to verify the accuracy of these reports.

Blindly, people have implemented those types of measures and they don't have any efficiency with them because they don't- you know, I mean, everybody is offering background checks now and stuff like that.
I'm afraid of is that people are implementing all these security security measures, but they don't really know if the reports are even accurate.
I have run my own background check to see what's in there.
I recently had an interview where I was going to be offered a really good job, but when it came to signing the paperwork for a background check, I had to disclose my felony.
I recently had an interview through my brother's connection, which went well until the background check paperwork was presented.
I know that I am a new guy, but they don't, so I think society's a lot less forgiving.
I'm a former criminal though.
Experienced racism and prejudice due to being Middle Eastern.
There is a lot stacked up against me.
Jobs are scarce, and it's all about who you know.
We live in a very politically charged city.
Feeling discouraged is a dangerous area for me.
Despite all the good things I have going for me, the only thing that's loud and clear are the things that I don't have going for me.
I'm getting sick of rejection and it's making me question the value of continuing my education.
Fear of returning to a lifestyle of crime, using, or destructive behavior.
I don't want to act out, I want to be productive.
I need to be centered to maintain composure and get ahead.
I want to make sure that I'm centered here so that I can go out there and do the best I can with maintaining composure, and then trying to get ahead.

content: I really wanna be a good productive member of society.
My hope is to strike up a conversation with someone who works for a company that does not have a policy against hiring people with felony convictions.
I would love to just be able to work for myself.
Strike up conversation with the right person
detail: At some point, I might strike up a conversation with the right person who would want to hire me regardless of my criminal history.

content: Continuing education and time since felony
detail: Continuing to go to school and allowing time to pass since the felony conviction.
It's been seven years since the felony conviction.
I've been clean from that sort of lifestyle for seven years.
I have the fact that I'm good at interaction with people, that's good.
I've been clean from that sort of lifestyle for that long so that's-that's-that's up for me, you know, that's-that's good.
I'm starting to work for myself, which is not a Fortune 500 company, but it provides some income.
It will all come together.
It's not a Fortune 500 company, but I have a little bit of income coming in from it.
I'm still continuing to go to school while starting my own business.
One of the things I read is to start your own business, so you don't have to report to anybody.
I can't work with kids or hazmat without a waiver.
Like truck driving and stuff like that.
I'm trying to look at the things that I can do.
The business is related to party planning and events, similar to wedding planning.
The business involves planning corporate events.
I'm involved in the planning of transportation from airports, hotel arrangements, and booking of rooms for corporate events.
Like corporate events when they need chauffeured transportation, limos, and hotel arrangements.
I, um, I'm involved in the planning of the transportation from airports, uh, hotel arrangements, um, uh, booking of, uh, you know, the rooms.
It's just, I guess just an event planner, but it's not through any sort of corporate, um, uh, company.
I am comfortable with working for myself and do well in the few opportunities I've had to organize events.
I decided to give it a shot despite the uncertainty.
An interview was given to me by my brother, who knew the guy.
The interview went great until the paperwork for a background check was presented.
My brother was not sure if a background check was involved.
He advised not to say anything until necessary.
I don't believe that I have a problem with the actual interview process.
It's been a lot.

My background is not being overlooked, making it difficult to move forward.

I hadn't anticipated the impact of the felony conviction; at the time, avoiding jail seemed like a relief.
I went back to school and got a degree, aiming for corporate or professional work.
It's more like a life sentence than a four-year sentence.
The felony conviction seems to knock me out of the ballpark when applying for jobs.
I don't get callbacks a lot because of that issue.
I interview extremely well, and it feels like the job is offered halfway through the interview, but when it comes to signing the paperwork, I disclose my felony, and it's like night and day.
I get the vibe that the job is offered almost halfway through the interview because, you know, they start telling me about what I'll be doing and what I'll be getting.
In situations where I don't know about it, I don't know about, I mean, if I'm not considered because of that, I probably-- Um, you know, that sucks too.

necessity_to_improve: I don't want to fall back.
detail: Having trouble dealing with my criminal history and trying to stay on the right path.
My background is not being overlooked, making it difficult to move forward.
My record has a felony conviction on it.
I hadn't anticipated the impact of the felony conviction; at the time, avoiding jail seemed like a relief.
I went back to school and got a degree, aiming for corporate or professional work.
It's more like a life sentence than a four-year sentence.
The felony conviction seems to knock me out of the ballpark when applying for jobs.
I don't get callbacks a lot because of that issue.
I interview extremely well, and it feels like the job is offered halfway through the interview, but when it comes to signing the paperwork, I disclose my felony, and it's like night and day.
I get the vibe that the job is offered almost halfway through the interview because, you know, they start telling me about what I'll be doing and what I'll be getting.
In situations where I don't know about it, I don't know about, I mean, if I'm not considered because of that, I probably-- Um, you know, that sucks too.
Black and white. It just stops right there. "I'm sorry. I can't really do much for you. I-- You have to go," you know, and it's—

When it comes to sign on the paperwork and I believe in honesty, so I tell them, you know, hey, look, this is what you're gonna find, you know, and, um, try to explain it. It's just-it's almost like night and day.
Most of the times, I interview extremely well.

The ones that really count are-are-are, you know, are kind of just, you know, uh, making me so angry and, you know, kind of spilling over into the rest of my life or the ones that I get face face, especially after a really good interview.
There are also times where I get an interview through some sort of connection or something, or like a buddy of mine or a family member will send me on an interview.
Sometimes they ask and, you know, I'll say something like, you know, "We'll discuss at interview".
Sometimes, there are situations where if I probably put that, I don't get a callback and those situations I don't know about.
It seems like it's a done deal, you just have to go through the motion of having the interview.
I don't have to fill out an application for interviews through connections; they just give you the interview.
After a great interview, when it comes to signing paperwork, my past conviction, which was non-violent and possession-related, becomes an issue.
The conviction was not violent or theft-related, but possession-related, and it happened seven or six years ago.
The conviction was non-violent and not a danger to others.
It's really frustrating because-
It's almost like I don't wanna give up, but it's like, I'm not given any other choice, you know?
If I disclose my history, I might get booted out right away.
Probably get booted out right away.
It's frustrating to get through a great interview, knowing I'm good for the job, and then get booted out.
If they get past it, then now I know that there's an actual intention by the hiring people to hire me.
If it's gonna make things more efficient, like weeding out the things, not wasting my time, then it's better.
A lot of the people that don't care about my criminal history are not the sort of jobs that I really wanna be doing.

Not like you're working for the government or for a bank or, you know, the FBI where- those are not things you're applying for. They're jobs where you would wonder why a-a non-violent felony would even be an issue.
Be an issue.
Anybody that I might tell that to that doesn't care are probably not jobs that are worth going to get the interview.

If I want to work at a moving company and I tell them off the bat, you know, from the gate, I tell them I have a felony conviction, just so you know before the interview and they don't really mind. I mean, that's great, you know, but at the same time, I don't- I'm not looking to be a mover.
I'm not looking to be a mover or a burger flipper or something like that. Not that there's anything wrong with it, but I feel like I have a little bit more to offer.
It's like being caught between a rock and a hard place.
I have done, and my eyes have, you know, just become like mush after all the reading I've done on this situation.
That's what it all boils down to is that I have a class one felony-

I found out that I have the type of felony that's expungable, I don't even have the type that's sealable. I have apparently the type that has to go on the governor's desk next to death row, you know, inmates or the candidates for a governor's pardon.

That needs to be, if any, you know, options, the only option I have is for governor's clemency and I can't figure out why because it's-it's, I mean, I just, I maybe there is a loophole somewhere, I don't know.
I've looked into a few groups that are supposedly helping convicted felons get work.
I think what I saw was a whole bunch of dead end jobs.
I found it really hard to get in and to get an interview with a counselor to match me up with a job.
It just didn't seem like a type of place that was willing to help.
The other thing that I saw was that it was very politically charged.
I saw that there was a lot of racial favor.
I didn't think that I was allowed to get an interview right away with one of those counselors that they offer.
I think that those types of groups are cool if they work. I just haven't had any luck working through that group.
At some point, I probably don't even have to report it, you know, and I have that going for me.
After the 9/11 attacks, many companies blindly implemented policies affecting criminal background checks, and there's no sure way to verify the accuracy of these reports.

Blindly, people have implemented those types of measures and they don't have any efficiency with them because they don't- you know, I mean, everybody is offering background checks now and stuff like that.
I'm afraid of is that people are implementing all these security security measures, but they don't really know if the reports are even accurate.
I have run my own background check to see what's in there.
I recently had an interview where I was going to be offered a really good job, but when it came to signing the paperwork for a background check, I had to disclose my felony.
I recently had an interview through my brother's connection, which went well until the background check paperwork was presented.
I know that I am a new guy, but they don't, so I think society's a lot less forgiving.
I'm a former criminal though.
I know. I've done some research on it for school.
It's a big cause of the recidivism rate.
It's really frustrating.
Experienced racism and prejudice due to being Middle Eastern.
There is a lot stacked up against me.
Jobs are scarce, and it's all about who you know.
We live in a very politically charged city.
Feeling discouraged is a dangerous area for me.
Despite all the good things I have going for me, the only thing that's loud and clear are the things that I don't have going for me.
I'm getting sick of rejection and it's making me question the value of continuing my education.
Fear of returning to a lifestyle of crime, using, or destructive behavior.
I don't want to act out, I want to be productive.
I need to be centered to maintain composure and get ahead.
I want to make sure that I'm centered here so that I can go out there and do the best I can with maintaining composure, and then trying to get ahead.
There is prejudicial motivation affecting opportunities.
It's really frustrating.

content: I really wanna be a good productive member of society.
My hope is to strike up a conversation with someone who works for a company that does not have a policy against hiring people with felony convictions.
I would love to just be able to work for myself.
Strike up conversation with the right person
detail: At some point, I might strike up a conversation with the right person who would want to hire me regardless of my criminal history.

content: Continuing education and time since felony
detail: Continuing to go to school and allowing time to pass since the felony conviction.
It's been seven years since the felony conviction.
I've been clean from that sort of lifestyle for seven years.
I have the fact that I'm good at interaction with people, that's good.
I've been clean from that sort of lifestyle for that long so that's-that's-that's up for me, you know, that's-that's good.
I'm starting to work for myself, which is not a Fortune 500 company, but it provides some income.
It will all come together.
It's not a Fortune 500 company, but I have a little bit of income coming in from it.
I'm still continuing to go to school while starting my own business.
One of the things I read is to start your own business, so you don't have to report to anybody.
I can't work with kids or hazmat without a waiver.
Like truck driving and stuff like that.
I'm trying to look at the things that I can do.
The business is related to party planning and events, similar to wedding planning.
The business involves planning corporate events.
I'm involved in the planning of transportation from airports, hotel arrangements, and booking of rooms for corporate events.
Like corporate events when they need chauffeured transportation, limos, and hotel arrangements.
I, um, I'm involved in the planning of the transportation from airports, uh, hotel arrangements, um, uh, booking of, uh, you know, the rooms.
It's just, I guess just an event planner, but it's not through any sort of corporate, um, uh, company.
I am comfortable with working for myself and do well in the few opportunities I've had to organize events.
I decided to give it a shot despite the uncertainty.
An interview was given to me by my brother, who knew the guy.
The interview went great until the paperwork for a background check was presented.
My brother was not sure if a background check was involved.
He advised not to say anything until necessary.
I don't believe that I have a problem with the actual interview process.
It's been a lot.

content: Finding a tactful way to inquire about hiring policies regarding criminal history.
Being forward about my criminal history to avoid wasting time and hope.
detail: Avoid being blunt about it, like asking 'Do you hire criminals?'
Consider being upfront about my felony conviction before proceeding with interviews.
confidence_to_achive: I think I might try this to spare myself frustration and further agony.

I think that I might try that. I think I might try, you know, from now on you know, uh, finding like a tactful way of asking, you know, I don't wanna be blunt about it and go you know, "Do you hire criminals?" or anything.
I think that's a really good idea to kind of like weed out, you know, but how do you say that?
I think it's probably better if I do tell them so I don't waste my time and hope.
I think I'm gonna try that from now on.

target.speaker: Counselor
target.concat_utterance: That's a great word for it there. Yeah. So, um-

n_contexts.speaker: Client
n_contexts.concat_utterance: Yeah, exactly.

n_contexts.speaker: Counselor
n_contexts.concat_utterance: Yeah, mm-hmm.

n_contexts.speaker: Client
n_contexts.concat_utterance: You know, I know there's-there's just a lot of- a lot of bullshit.

n_contexts.speaker: Counselor
n_contexts.concat_utterance: Mm-hmm.

n_contexts.concat_utterance: Yeah.

harm_effect: It's overwhelming and causing anger and depression to resurface.
The ones that really count are making me so angry and spilling over into the rest of my life.
necessity_to_improve: I don't want to fall back.
detail: Having trouble dealing with my criminal history and trying to stay on the right path.
My background is not being overlooked, making it difficult to move forward.
My record has a felony conviction on it.
I hadn't anticipated the impact of the felony conviction; at the time, avoiding jail seemed like a relief.
I went back to school and got a degree, aiming for corporate or professional work.
It's more like a life sentence than a four-year sentence.
The felony conviction seems to knock me out of the ballpark when applying for jobs.
I don't get callbacks a lot because of that issue.
I interview extremely well, and it feels like the job is offered halfway through the interview, but when it comes to signing the paperwork, I disclose my felony, and it's like night and day.
I get the vibe that the job is offered almost halfway through the interview because, you know, they start telling me about what I'll be doing and what I'll be getting.
In situations where I don't know about it, I don't know about, I mean, if I'm not considered because of that, I probably-- Um, you know, that sucks too.
Black and white. It just stops right there. "I'm sorry. I can't really do much for you. I-- You have to go," you know, and it's—

When it comes to sign on the paperwork and I believe in honesty, so I tell them, you know, hey, look, this is what you're gonna find, you know, and, um, try to explain it. It's just-it's almost like night and day.
Most of the times, I interview extremely well.

The ones that really count are-are-are, you know, are kind of just, you know, uh, making me so angry and, you know, kind of spilling over into the rest of my life or the ones that I get face face, especially after a really good interview.
There are also times where I get an interview through some sort of connection or something, or like a buddy of mine or a family member will send me on an interview.
Sometimes they ask and, you know, I'll say something like, you know, "We'll discuss at interview".
Sometimes, there are situations where if I probably put that, I don't get a callback and those situations I don't know about.
It seems like it's a done deal, you just have to go through the motion of having the interview.
I don't have to fill out an application for interviews through connections; they just give you the interview.
After a great interview, when it comes to signing paperwork, my past conviction, which was non-violent and possession-related, becomes an issue.
The conviction was not violent or theft-related, but possession-related, and it happened seven or six years ago.
The conviction was non-violent and not a danger to others.
It's really frustrating because-
It's almost like I don't wanna give up, but it's like, I'm not given any other choice, you know?
If I disclose my history, I might get booted out right away.
Probably get booted out right away.
It's frustrating to get through a great interview, knowing I'm good for the job, and then get booted out.
If they get past it, then now I know that there's an actual intention by the hiring people to hire me.
If it's gonna make things more efficient, like weeding out the things, not wasting my time, then it's better.
A lot of the people that don't care about my criminal history are not the sort of jobs that I really wanna be doing.

Not like you're working for the government or for a bank or, you know, the FBI where- those are not things you're applying for. They're jobs where you would wonder why a-a non-violent felony would even be an issue.
Be an issue.
Anybody that I might tell that to that doesn't care are probably not jobs that are worth going to get the interview.

If I want to work at a moving company and I tell them off the bat, you know, from the gate, I tell them I have a felony conviction, just so you know before the interview and they don't really mind. I mean, that's great, you know, but at the same time, I don't- I'm not looking to be a mover.
I'm not looking to be a mover or a burger flipper or something like that. Not that there's anything wrong with it, but I feel like I have a little bit more to offer.
It's like being caught between a rock and a hard place.
I have done, and my eyes have, you know, just become like mush after all the reading I've done on this situation.
That's what it all boils down to is that I have a class one felony-

I found out that I have the type of felony that's expungable, I don't even have the type that's sealable. I have apparently the type that has to go on the governor's desk next to death row, you know, inmates or the candidates for a governor's pardon.

That needs to be, if any, you know, options, the only option I have is for governor's clemency and I can't figure out why because it's-it's, I mean, I just, I maybe there is a loophole somewhere, I don't know.
I've looked into a few groups that are supposedly helping convicted felons get work.
I think what I saw was a whole bunch of dead end jobs.
I found it really hard to get in and to get an interview with a counselor to match me up with a job.
It just didn't seem like a type of place that was willing to help.
The other thing that I saw was that it was very politically charged.
I saw that there was a lot of racial favor.
I didn't think that I was allowed to get an interview right away with one of those counselors that they offer.
I think that those types of groups are cool if they work. I just haven't had any luck working through that group.
At some point, I probably don't even have to report it, you know, and I have that going for me.
After the 9/11 attacks, many companies blindly implemented policies affecting criminal background checks, and there's no sure way to verify the accuracy of these reports.

Blindly, people have implemented those types of measures and they don't have any efficiency with them because they don't- you know, I mean, everybody is offering background checks now and stuff like that.
I'm afraid of is that people are implementing all these security security measures, but they don't really know if the reports are even accurate.
I have run my own background check to see what's in there.
I recently had an interview where I was going to be offered a really good job, but when it came to signing the paperwork for a background check, I had to disclose my felony.
I recently had an interview through my brother's connection, which went well until the background check paperwork was presented.
I know that I am a new guy, but they don't, so I think society's a lot less forgiving.
I'm a former criminal though.
I know. I've done some research on it for school.
It's a big cause of the recidivism rate.
It's really frustrating.
There's a lot of bullshit.
Rejection has affected my mental state.
Experienced racism and prejudice due to being Middle Eastern.
There is a lot stacked up against me.
Jobs are scarce, and it's all about who you know.
We live in a very politically charged city.
Feeling discouraged is a dangerous area for me.
Despite all the good things I have going for me, the only thing that's loud and clear are the things that I don't have going for me.
I'm getting sick of rejection and it's making me question the value of continuing my education.
Fear of returning to a lifestyle of crime, using, or destructive behavior.
I don't want to act out, I want to be productive.
I need to be centered to maintain composure and get ahead.
I want to make sure that I'm centered here so that I can go out there and do the best I can with maintaining composure, and then trying to get ahead.
There is prejudicial motivation affecting opportunities.
It's really frustrating.

content: I really wanna be a good productive member of society.

I would love to just be able to work for myself.

It will all come together.

I decided to give it a shot despite the uncertainty.
An interview was given to me by my brother, who knew the guy.
The interview went great until the paperwork for a background check was presented.

I don't believe that I have a problem with the actual interview process.
It's been a lot.
